questions.prompt: Tell me about a time you had to learn something quickly to deliver work.
options: I broke the skill into daily goals, shipped a smaller first milestone, and documented gaps for follow-up.
I stayed late and figured it out eventually.
I asked a teammate to do it for me so we could move faster.
I avoided that task and focused on things I already knew.

questions.prompt: How do you handle conflict with a coworker?
options: I align on shared goals, clarify assumptions, and agree on a concrete next step.
I ignore it and hope it goes away.
I escalate immediately before talking to them.
I prove I am right in front of the team.

questions.prompt: Describe a project you are proud of.
options: I explain the impact, constraints, what I owned, and what I would improve next time.
It was great because everyone said so.
I mostly did whatever my manager told me.
I cannot think of one.

questions.prompt: How do you prioritize when everything feels urgent?
options: I rank by business impact, risk, and deadlines, then communicate tradeoffs early.
I do whichever task is easiest first.
I wait for reminders from stakeholders.
I multitask all tasks at once.

questions.prompt: Tell me about a time you made a mistake.
options: I owned it quickly, fixed root cause, and added a safeguard to prevent repeats.
I did not make mistakes on important projects.
I blamed unclear requirements.
I fixed it silently so nobody would notice.

questions.prompt: How do you receive critical feedback?
options: I ask clarifying questions, summarize action items, and follow up with results.
I defend my original approach until people agree.
I take it personally and disengage.
I nod but usually ignore it.

questions.prompt: How do you collaborate with non-technical teammates?
options: I translate technical choices into user impact and keep updates short and regular.
I only discuss implementation details.
I let my manager handle communication.
I avoid meetings and send long docs later.

questions.prompt: Describe a time you improved a process.
options: I found a bottleneck, proposed a small experiment, measured results, and rolled it out.
I complained until someone else changed it.
I made an undocumented change and moved on.
I left the process alone because change is risky.
Solving meaningful problems, learning fast, and delivering measurable value to users.
Only promotions and titles.
Avoiding difficult tasks.
Doing exactly the minimum needed.

questions.prompt: Why are you interested in this role?
options: The role matches my strengths, has clear product impact, and offers room to grow.
I am applying everywhere without reading job descriptions.
I only care about a short commute.
I mostly want less responsibility.
